text: iOS 上我做的那个 HTML 和 Markdown 预览的 App （即览）差不多了，感觉晚上就能给大家发测试资格了 https://t.co/cMdAzMrEGL

Your prompt isn't broken. One word inside it is.

Same structure. Same model. Same context. Change one word and the output shifts completely.

Most people treat synonyms as interchangeable inside prompts. LLMs don't. Here are 3 common ones that mean very different things to an https://t.co/HNDeEWYBOv

"Explain" vs. "Describe"

These feel identical. They're not.

"Explain RAG to me" gets you retrieval mechanics, why chunks are embedded, how context windows are populated, and where the architecture breaks.

"Describe RAG to me" gets you a surface-level overview. What it looks

"Professional" vs. "Formal"

This one causes the most pain.

You type "write this in a formal tone" and wonder why the output reads like a legal notice.

You meant "professional." Clear, competent, workplace-sharp.

"Formal" triggers ceremony. Stiff structure. Elevated register.

"Detailed" vs. "Comprehensive"

"Give me a detailed report" and "Give me a comprehensive report" sound like the same request.

One goes deep. The other goes wide.

"Detailed" tells the model to expand on each point with depth, nuance, and specifics.
"Comprehensive" tells the

Now here's what matters more than any of this.

One precise word helps. But a sentence of context beats a perfect synonym every time.

Instead of agonizing over "formal" vs. "professional," just write: "Write this for a workplace audience. Clear, confident, no stiff language."

Anthropic just gave Claude Code a "don't stop until it's done" mode.

/goal lets you set a completion condition, walk away, and Claude keeps iterating across turns until the condition is met. Hours. Sometimes days.

The capability is real. So is the bill.
